text: Wow, the circles of evaluation thing is awesome, thanks so much for linking it! I haven't seen that before but it's definitely the closest thing I've seen to the visual layout approach I'm using in this project, so that's really cool to know about

elements.text: Wow, the circles of evaluation thing is awesome, thanks so much for linking it! I haven't seen that before but it's definitely the closest thing I've seen to the visual layout approach I'm using in this project, so that's really cool to know about

text: When the program is invalid it'll just continue running the previous program until it becomes valid again, there's no special fault-tolerant semantics or anything like that.

elements.text: When the program is invalid it'll just continue running the previous program until it becomes valid again, there's no special fault-tolerant semantics or anything like that.

text:
Right now there's no way to see intermediate values but I definitely appreciate what you're suggesting and I plan to do some things in that direction eventually.

Currently easl is just a shader language and so it only runs on the GPU, so tracking intermediate values and stuff would be difficult in that context. But the plan is to also have a CPU-hosted interpreter for the language that can be used for testing and debugging, and more interactive development with a repl and whatnot. So once that's done you'll be able to write a program that uses a `(print ...)` function, such that that function gets ignored when run on the GPU but actually prints things to a little debug window/corner when run on the CPU (or just the terminal if you use the language outside of this IDE, ofc).

In the long-run the idea is that the CPU-side interpreter will also be able to orchestrate calls to the GPU-side, e.g. there'll be a higher-order function `run-fragment-shader` that accepts a fragment shader as an argument and dispatches it on the GPU. That way you'll be able to build much more sophisticated pipelines involving multiple passes of vertex/fragment/compute shaders alongside arbitrary CPU-side logic, as opposed to right now where you only control a single fragment shader and therefore can only really write a pure function from position+time to color. Anyways, my point in saying all this is that once that's all working, it'll be possible to implement something like "hover over point in final image and see values" yourself, by just defining a fragment function that uses `print` in the relevant places, then using that same function both as an argument to `run-fragment-shader` and by directly it on the CPU with the coordinates of the user's mouse.

text: I guess all that is to say is that my goal for now is to make this as powerful and customizable as possible in user-space, as opposed to trying to build in a bunch of hard-coded convenience features myself.

elements.text: I guess all that is to say is that my goal for now is to make this as powerful and customizable as possible in user-space, as opposed to trying to build in a bunch of hard-coded convenience features myself.

text: And re "select expression circle and see image of what it returned for each point", one thing I've had in mind that would serve that purpose would be to have a kind of GUI-expression that  has a hole inside where you place some code, and it displays a preview of the result of that code right there in a little box inside the expression. So this would be a persistent part of the structure of the program, as opposed to something that you'd only see when you select the expression, but it would serve a similar purpose to what you're suggesting I think.

elements.text: And re "select expression circle and see image of what it returned for each point", one thing I've had in mind that would serve that purpose would be to have a kind of GUI-expression that  has a hole inside where you place some code, and it displays a preview of the result of that code right there in a little box inside the expression. So this would be a persistent part of the structure of the program, as opposed to something that you'd only see when you select the expression, but it would serve a similar purpose to what you're suggesting I think.

text: Here's a little diagram to explain what I mean, if this helps. This could be used anywhere in the program, even nested inside one another, to help you visualize the intermediate values of your code and watch them as you change other stuff.

elements.text: Here's a little diagram to explain what I mean, if this helps. This could be used anywhere in the program, even nested inside one another, to help you visualize the intermediate values of your code and watch them as you change other stuff.

text: Sorta analogous to how in touchdesigner you see each intermediate buffer used in your program displayed in a little rectangle

elements.text: Sorta analogous to how in touchdesigner you see each intermediate buffer used in your program displayed in a little rectangle

text: And again the hope is that it'll be possible to implement that kind of GUI-expression yourself within the IDE, it won't have to be a built-in feature. Ofc there'll be a nice "standard library" of GUI elements like this so the user isn't left reimplementing basic stuff on their own, but having this kind of thing being expressible from within userspace is important to me, so that people can build their own crazy abstractions on top of it all. The ultimate hope is that this will be something like the emacs of GUI/graphics programming :grin:

text:
Cool!  I have a toy stack language i want to convert to function of x,y and eventually from cpu to shaders, so it's helpful to hear how you're approaching that ;-)

The in-source persistent visualizing forms sound awesome :+1: Good marriage of "i'm a print debugger" + livelits.
